data.title: Generation PMCA Q3 2025 Quarterly
data.description:
Long Live Value Investing

Some have argued that value investing is dead. Growth names have considerably outperformed. Passive (index-linked) investments now represent the majority of ETFs and mutual funds. These two phenomena have been reinforcing because the growth companies—pricier companies whose valuations tend to overshoot as growth is prized—represent such a disproportionate portion of major indices. Because information is so readily available, markets are highly efficient and many contend that markets no longer produce meaningful anomalies. As such, strategies focused on value have been regarded as outdated.

But claims of the death of value investing are always prevalent near market tops, when speculation has unduly lifted securities, seemingly without reference to underlying value. Bitcoin is up because bitcoin is up. Gold is way up. What kind of “safe haven” is as volatile as stocks? And it trades about 2.7x its cost of production—a level only briefly reached by 4 other commodities in the last 50 years, all of which promptly sold off dramatically. Many stocks are also ignoring underlying fundamentals. Anything AI related has been raging.

Active managers generally underperform underlying stock indexes. It’s mainly because indexes are designed to include the strongest companies—most are cap weighted, overweighting the largest ones—while weaklings are culled. No fees or commissions in indexes helps too. Over the last 20 years, fewer than 2% of all U.S. mutual funds have managed to beat the S&P 500. As such, why would investors not simply passively invest in index ETFs?

Stacking the Deck in Our Favour

To us, the answer is simple; by employing the tenets of value investing we can buy high-quality undervalued securities that are being ignored, while avoiding poor quality or expensive ones. Therefore, our process begins with specifically designed screens of leading securities and/or markets to distill a list of those that are likely to outperform. Then, these positions are assessed utilizing our TRAC™ tool to optimize entry and exit points.

Some assert that stock prices alwaysreflect the value of companies. But prices fluctuate way too much to constantly be indicative of value. Most academics argue markets are mostly efficient, and we agree; however, inefficiencies certainly exist. Though, in our wired world, inefficiencies don’t last as long. Therefore, we need to react relatively quickly when an opportunity presents itself, and similarly when a position reverts to our estimate of its Fair Market Value (FMV).

Indexes are currently positioned to underperform. Markets have been more efficient than usual leaving fewer companies undervalued. Some have traded too high above their underlying values, pushing overall indexes too high, right to ceilings in our work.

In the short term, market psychology drives prices. Over time, economic reality prevails and prices coincide with values derived from businesses’ bottom-lines. That’s right out of the value investing handbook. Value investing isn’t dead; it’s just been overshadowed.

Weakening Fundamentals

Across the U.S. and Canada, construction is contracting for housing and commercial buildings. You don’t need to tell Toronto condo builders there’s a contraction. Sales activity is 90% below the 10-year average and prices per square foot are down over 22% from their 2022 peak.

Lenders generally have become anxious. For example, lenders are changing terms to require unanimous consent before borrowers can cause a new loan to rank ahead of theirs. At the rate this is occurring—over 80% of all credit deals—it’s possibly an early-warning sign about impeding credit issues. Delinquencies are already up for consumers and small businesses, while sub-prime borrowers are feeling an even greater pinch. Business bankruptcies are running high too.

In the first 3 quarters of 2025, companies cut 55% more jobs than the comparable period last year, the most since the 2020 recession. October’s job cuts were the most for October since 2003. Consumer sentiment is awful and, based on history, is foreshadowing much more unemployment.

Capacity utilization has weakened globally, down to about 75% from its peak of 79%. This likely means additional idle capacity, lower capital spending, increased corporate financial pressures, and potential price cuts. While it doesn’t auger well for economic growth, it implies lower inflation. Disinflationary pressures should also continue from AI—a productivity enhancer and expense reducer—as well as tariffs which inhibit global trade, and other growth suppressors such as high government debt levels, contracting money supply, and anemic population growth. As a result, lower interest rates are likely.

The economy has been buoyed by spending on AI infrastructure which has been astounding, though it is unlikely to last at its current level. U.S. IT spending has risen to nearly 4.5% of GDP, just below where it peaked in the dot-com bubble. Further evidence that it’s overdone, Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOGL), and Meta (META) have spent 10 times what they’re receiving in AI revenue this year, and profits, if any, would be negligible. These companies are now spending over 60% of their cash flow on capital expenditures, up from a more normal average of 30%. And we won’t even delve into the complications around the key players who seem to be funding each other, i.e. investing in/financing customers.

GDP growth rates have already fallen and profits margins nearly always follow too but, unusually, margins have increased for the past year or so.

Competition from China is coming. Though U.S. protectionism could slow its onset, misplaced U.S. policies are forcing lasting structural changes. The political climate has led many Chinese born academics and tech experts to flee the U.S. and return to China. Already, China produces many products more efficiently and much cheaper. Now, with better funding, more R&D, and operating in a more competitive fashion, with more urgency, it could catapult ahead. China is responsible for half the world's most-cited scientific papers, compared to a decline to one-third from the U.S. China could take the lead in key areas such as AI, quantum computing, and biotech.

Priced for Perfection

Yet, major U.S. and Canadian stock markets are close to all-time highs, at or below TRAC™ ceilings, and priced above FMVs, leaving them vulnerable to price declines. Long-term attractiveness is poor too, since real returns (net of inflation) have never been positive over a 10-year period when equity valuations have been this high. The ratio of the Wilshire 5000 (the broadest measure of U.S. corporate prices) to after-tax profits has only been higher during the dot-com bubble.

The stock markets don’t seem to care. Market speculation has continued. Equity call option volume relative to puts—bullish bets versus bearish—is the highest on record. Equity ownership is at all-time highs, itself implying poor 10-year expected returns. And the Russell 2000 small cap companies that are unprofitable were up 30% in 6 weeks through mid-October, while high-quality stocks’ underperformance is at a low not seen since the dot-com bubble.

Given that valuations are so high, U.S. growth is weakening, and our Economic Composite, TEC™, is still alerting us to a potential recession, we continue to maintain hedging strategies by either holding short positions (where authorized) or inverse long ETFs.

Our Model Portfolios

Our managed accounts are invested based on one or more of our Models (particular investment strategies with notional allocations of securities). A managed account’s holdings will generally be similar to its applicable Model’s, but may not hold all of them based on client-specific factors (income requirements, tax-related considerations, requests/restrictions, and cash available for purchases) and/or market forces which impact specific investment decisions from time to time.

The following descriptions of the holdings in our managed accounts are intended only to explain the reasons that we have made, and continue to hold, these investments in the accounts we manage for you and are not intended as advice or recommendations with respect to purchasing, selling or holding the securities described. Below, we discuss each of our new holdings and updates on key holdings if there have been material developments.

All Cap Model

The All Cap Model combines selections from our large cap strategy (Global Insight) with our small and medium cap ideas. We generally prefer large cap companies for their superior liquidity and lower volatility. The smaller cap positions tend to be less liquid and more volatile; however, we may hold these positions where they are cheaper, trading at relatively greater discounts to our FMV estimates, making their risk/reward profiles favourable.

There were no recent material changes in our smaller cap holdings, and no smaller cap purchases or sales. Our large cap positions are summarized in the Global Insight section.

Global Insight (Large Cap) Model

Global Insight portfolios hold large cap stocks (typically with market caps over $5 billion at the time of purchase but may include those in the $2-5 billion range) where portfolios are managed Long/Short or Long only. At an average of less than 70 cents-on-the-dollar versus our FMV estimates, our Global Insight holdings appear much cheaper, in aggregate, than the overall market.

We adjusted our lululemon (LULU) FMV estimate down to $260 to reflect the impact from U.S. tariffs. While we expected some margin erosion from tariffs, we did not expect the removal of the de minimis exemptions (one could receive products in the U.S. with a value of less than $800 without duties). lululemon itself wasn’t expecting that removal, if at all, until 2027. And the impact is widespread since 92% of all cargo shipped to the U.S. (about 4 million packages a day) apparently fell under de minimis exemptions prior to the removal in August. Approximately two-thirds of lululemon’s online U.S. shipments were being fulfilled by its Canadian warehouses. The company already has plans to offset half the impact of the additional tariffs and de minimis exemption removal. Potential price increases could further assist in alleviating these cost pressures. It also blames itself for being too lax with respect to new product launches, so that should be revitalized going forward too. The company is adding about 40 new stores annually, or over 5% per year to its store base. Its return on invested capital remains way above the average company, even with its depressed forward earnings base, which should be a temporary low point.

In the last few months, we have made several changes in our large-cap positions. We bought Adobe (ADBE), Blue Owl Capital (OWL), and Wise. We sold Marvell Technology (MRVL), Uber Technologies (UBER), Universal Health Services (UHS), and Warner Brothers (WBD) after each ran up TRAC™ceilings near our FMV estimate and we sold Alibaba Health Information Technology, Ping An Insurance, and Alibaba Group as each broke below TRAC™ floors.

Adobe provides productivity and creative software for businesses, creators, and marketing professionals. Key products include Acrobat, Express, Firefly, and Creative Cloud. Naysayers have argued that artificial intelligence apps such as Sora and Nano Banana could replace the need for Adobe’s creative suite. We believe Adobe’s products should remain the gold standard for creative work since its suite is the only solution that synthesizes commercially safe AI with powerful tools for human input, options for custom art, video, and music, and final formatting for print, video, web, and social media. We view AI as a growth driver, not an existential threat. Top-line growth should be in the high-single-digits and free cash flow could hit $10 billion this fiscal year. Our FMV estimate is $440.

Blue Owl Capital is a rapidly growing U.S. asset manager with over $250 billion under management. Its funds operate primarily in direct lending with holdings also in various General Partnerships and private REITs. Almost all its fee revenue is from permanent capital, because of multi-year lockups or indefinite holding periods in the case of its listed Business Development Companies or GP stakes. While the stock has fallen because the market has become concerned with private credit risks generally, the company underwrites its loans to Great Recession conditions, the average loan-to-value is only 39%, and historical credit losses have been a mere 11 basis points. Direct lending should continue to capture market share from banks and other traditional lenders. Blue Owl trades well below our estimated FMV of $27 and yields 6.4%.

Wise is a UK-based financial services company whose products have been designed to offer a faster, cheaper, foreign exchange solution for retail and business customers. Where banks often charge 2-3% for cross-border transactions, which are burdened with administration, Wise transfers average only 52 basis points (which it keeps reducing) and over 74% of its transactions are instantaneous. A Wise VISA debit card is a useful tool for any international traveler. Over 70% of new customers have been generated by word of mouth but the company is now marketing aggressively to further advance its already robust growth. Earnings are expected to continue to grow by 15-20% annually. The company should not be susceptible to competition from Stablecoins, which are geared to one currency. Wise aims to minimize competitive threats by increasing the number of countries where it’s registered under regulations to operate, onboarding major partners, and lowering prices. Our FMV estimate is £13.5.

Multifaceted Diversification

We also aim to limit volatility and drawdowns by combining investment strategies, especially where returns are less correlated. The goal is to outperform through economic cycles with low correlation, therefore less susceptibility, to market index declines. We can construct portfolios with multiple unique return drivers—strategies that differ in style and approach—based on bottom-up fundamentals, macro tools, or pure quantitative analysis. This can provide exposure to different styles and asset classes beyond just stock and bond indexes.

The benefits of multifaceted diversification are not only from different ways to perform but also from a portfolio comprised of strategies that are less correlated. So that when a strategy underperforms, it’s less likely to occur at the same time as another strategy, which softens the volatility and drawdowns of the overall investment portfolio.

Global Tactical Allocation Model

Our Global Tactical Allocation Model (GTAM) investment process combines macroeconomic analysis with valuation and momentum. ETFs (exchange traded funds) are used to gain exposure to 4 broad asset classes: Equities—major markets, emerging markets, sectors, styles, private equity; Fixed Income—bonds issued by governments, investment grade corporations, high-yield issuers, as well as mortgages, and bond indexes; Real Assets—real estate, infrastructure, renewable energy; and Commodities—Precious Metals or Oil. GTAM emphasizes ETFs that should outperform based on the macro environment, are selling at attractive absolute and relative valuations, possess good relative price momentum, and are at TRAC™ floors.

Current exposures are: Equities (44%); Fixed Income (31%); Real Assets (23%); and Commodities (none). Its current broad themes are emerging markets, U.S. real estate, U.S. consumer staples, and U.S. healthcare. The consumer staples and healthcare sectors are undervalued and sitting at TRAC™ floors. In fixed income, long-term U.S. bonds were sold after rallying on rate cut anticipation.

Quantitative Investment Models

Quantitative equity strategies commonly select securities based on systematic, rules-based decisions, using technology to uncover and exploit historical statistically significant anomalies. Our quantitative equity strategies employ proprietary and systematic processes that rank large cap stocks based on factors such as relative valuation, operating metrics (quality), financial strength, and price momentum. The two models noted below select approximately 40 holdings from the top-ranked stocks in the model’s respective universe. Portfolios are rebalanced every 6 months with interim partial rebalancing. TRAC™ is also utilized to optimize entry and exit points.

The Quantitative Global Value Model (QGVM) invests in large-cap equities from around the world. The U.S., Canada, and Japan currently represent the top three countries. The top 3 sectors are Energy (19%), Industrials (16%) and Communication Services (9%). QGVM has a price-to-earnings ratio of 15x, much lower than 23.6x for the ACWI. The model’s dividend yield is 3.4% versus the ACWI’s 1.6%.

The Quantitative Canadian Value Model (QCVM) restricts its universe to Canada’s S&P/TSX Composite. The top 3 sectors are currently Materials (25%), Energy (16%), and Industrials (11%). After an incredible multi-year run, most precious metals companies are at or near TRAC™ ceilings; we expect to rotate out of our precious metal positions and use proceeds to invest in more undervalued opportunities. We believe QCVM has similar quality metrics as the S&P/TSX, yet on average, QCVM’s holdings have a lower valuation (median forward P/E of QCVM is 12.4x, versus the S&P/TSX constituents 15.1x) and a higher dividend yield (3.1% compared to 2.3% for the index).

Income Model

Our high-yield investment strategy has an average current annual yield (income we receive as a percent of current market value of income securities held) of about 5.4%, and most of our holdings—corporate bonds/debentures, preferred shares, REITs, and high-yielding common shares—trade below our FMV estimates.

U.S. high-yield corporate bonds (ICE BofA Index) yield 6.8%. Long-term U.S. government bond yields have remained higher than most expect because inflation remains higher than bondholders prefer. High-yield corporate bond spreads are also quite narrow relative to history. We expect a widening, especially if the economy weakens and corporate delinquencies escalate.

We recently purchased Blue Owl Capital (described above under Global Insight) and XIOR Student Housing, Europe’s largest student housing company because we were attracted by its 100% occupancy rate, 5% steady rental growth rate, new projects, discount to our €39 estimated FMV, and 6% dividend yield. We sold Northland Power (NPI:CA) and HA Sustainable Infrastructure (HASI), as both rose to TRAC™ ceilings in line with our FMV estimates.

Investment Grade Income Model

Our investment grade strategy utilizes a systematic process to rank Canadian investment-grade rated corporate bonds based on their duration, yield, financial strength, and momentum.

Currently, positioning has emphasized longer-dated bonds—duration is 10.6 years, 5 years more than the S&P Canada Investment Grade Corporate Bond Index. The average yield-to-maturity is 5.1% versus 4.1% for the index.

A Rare Breed

As indexes run higher and passive investing gains even more converts, value investors are becoming scarce. But bottom-up security selection based on value and quality, while not easy, should be sought out and embraced, especially when market valuations are so high.

Value investing uses common sense. We endeavour to buy at a discount and sell at fair market value. We emphasize quality businesses, diversify holdings, and hedge via shorting or inverse longs when our models indicate a recession or bear market. We can diversify across strategies too, for exposure to additional assets classes or geographies which ought to reduce drawdowns and volatility. None of that sounds like it should be scarce.

The best investment ideas tend to come from well-sourced idea generation. We continue to integrate all our tools to screen for the best new potential investments, to constantly parse and analyze the results, and to find appropriate entry and exit points for those selected for inclusion.

Underlying fundamentals ultimately matter, even though psychology can overwhelm for some time. Just like their investments, value investors themselves are currently ignored. But time and logic are on our side. We’re nowhere near extinction.

Long live value investing.

Randall Abramson, CFA

All investments involve risk, including loss of principal. This document provides information not intended to meet objectives or suitability requirements of any specific individual. This information is provided for educational or discussion purposes only and should not be considered investment advice or a solicitation to buy or sell securities. The information contained herein has been drawn from sources which we believe to be reliable; however, its accuracy or completeness is not guaranteed. This report is not to be construed as an offer, solicitation or recommendation to buy or sell any of the securities herein named. We may or may not continue to hold any of the securities mentioned. Generation PMCA Corp., its affiliates and/or their respective officers, directors, employees or shareholders may from time to time acquire, hold or sell securities named in this report. It should not be assumed that any of the securities transactions or holdings discussed were or will prove to be profitable, or that the investment decisions we make in the future will be profitable or will equal the investment performance of the securities discussed herein. E.&O.E.

data.title: Main Street Capital: What Bubble?
data.description:
Investors love to look at Main Street Capital’s (MAIN) relatively high price-to-book value and immediately declare it an overvalued bubble of a Business Development Company (“BDC”). But what many investors don’t realize is it’s a fundamentally different type of BDC given its steady net asset value (“NAV”) gains (while peers stay relatively flat—or worse). So while some thumb their nose at Main Street’s mere 5.4% dividend yield (puny in the BDC space), they fail to recognize the steady dividend growth, plus the powerful supplemental dividends, as well as the many other attractive qualities that set it apart (i.e. low debt, strong liquidity, above-average pipeline, healthy portfolio, efficient operations and macro resilience). This report reviews all of that (and the big risks) and then concludes with my strong opinion on investing.

Main Street Capital Facts

Main Street Capital Corporation is a BDC providing long-term debt and equity capital to lower middle-market (“LMM”) companies (those with annual revenues between $10 million and $150 million) as well as debt to private equity-backed firms. It invests across diverse industries and its internal management structure (which allows for more efficient operations) makes it special.

Additionally, as a regulated investment company (“RIC”), it distributes at least 90% its income as dividends to avoid corporate taxes, and also thereby enabling those big steady monthly dividends many investors love.

Importantly, Main Street’s focus on the lower middle market provides a differentiated competitive advantage by addressing an underserved, fragmented segment where larger BDCs and institutions often overlook opportunities (and thereby allowing MAIN to secure attractive risk-adjusted returns through its specialized niche expertise).

Main Street Vs. Other BDCs

A lot of investors avoid Main Street because its current dividend yield is relatively low in the BDC space and its price-to-book value (a basic BDC valuation metric) is relatively high (see table below).

Compare MAIN with (ARCC) (OBDC) (BXSL) (FSK) (GBDC) (PSEC) (OCSL) (CSWC) (TRIN).

However, a lot of investors fail to recognize many of the unique and attractive qualities that make these Main Street metrics not only acceptable, but actually quite attractive, as will be reviewed in the paragraphs below.

Growing NAV

Unlike a lot of its peers, Main Street’s business approach has allowed it to steadily grow its net asset value over time (as you can see in the chart below).

And a growing NAV (or “book value”) also allows it to increase the dividend (e.g., a higher asset base upon which to earn income). And encouragingly, the most recent third quarter marked the 13th consecutive quarter of record NAV per share (see table chart above). It also has an impressive 17% annualized ROE (return on equity) which underscores its long-term value creation for investors.

Dividend Health

With both regular dividends (paid monthly) and supplemental dividends, Main Street is a dividend powerhouse. In fact, the board just declared a $0.30 supplemental dividend (payable Dec 2025—it’s 17th consecutive quarter) and raised the Q1 2026 monthly dividend 4% YoY to $0.26/share. And notably, trailing 12-month supplemental dividends total $1.20/share (40% above regular), with expectation for another significant March 2026 supplement tied to excess DNII (distributable net investment income) and realized gains.

Also, Q3 DNII before taxes was $1.07 per share (guidance ≥$1.05 Q4), which supports dividend coverage even if nonrecurring income (recently $4.3M in Q3) moderates.

For a little more perspective, you can see weighted average interest coupon on Main Street’s existing LMM debt investments in the table below (it’s basically well above the actual dividend yield, which demonstrates how they generate the income, but also that they have plenty of cushion built into the business).

Efficient Operations and Cost Structure

As an internally-managed BDC, Main Street has a significant operating cost advantage (see chart below) plus lower perceived conflicts of interest versus externally managed BDCs.

For example, “non-interest operating expenditures” versus average assets is only 1.4% on an annualized basis—among the industry lowest.

Conservative Leverage and Strong Liquidity

As you can see in the earlier BDC comparison table, Main Street has lower debt-to-equity than peers (which basically gives it more cushion if the market gets ugly, or more dry powder for when compelling opportunities arise). Further, “regulatory debt-to-equity” was 0.62x (below 0.8–0.9x target) with $1.5B+ liquidity (post the recent $350M 5.4% notes issuance and $150M early repayment). This puts Main Street in a position of strength (and able to take advantage of pipeline opportunities), especially considering most of its debt is first lien (safer) anyway (as you can see in the chart below).

Above-Average Investment Pipeline

According to Main Street CEO, Dwayne Hyzak, on the most recent quarterly call, the BDC’s pipeline is above average:

“Now turning to our current investment pipeline. As of today, I would characterize our lower middle market investment pipeline as above average. Consistent with our experience in prior periods of broad economic uncertainty, we believe that our ability to provide unique and flexible financing solutions to lower middle market companies and their owners and management teams and our differentiated long-term to permanent holding periods represent an even more attractive solution to the needs of many lower middle market companies given the current economic environment, and we are confident in our expectations for strong lower middle market investment activity in the fourth quarter.”

This encouraging environment (supported by Main Street healthy liquidity and low credit) is also supported by recent LMM portfolio outperformance and exit potential. For example, according to Hyzak (on the most recently quarterly call):

“We also continue to see significant interest from potential buyers in several of our lower middle market portfolio companies, which we expect will lead to favorable realizations over the next few quarters and which we believe further highlights the strength and quality of our portfolio companies and their exceptional leadership teams.”

These are investments with strong dividend income and fair value appreciation from positive performance. Main Street’s pipeline is healthy.

Asset Management Business Momentum

Important to mention, Main Street also has a healthy asset management business. Specifically, Main Street is the investment adviser and administrator to two private funds, each of which is solely focused on MAIN’s Private Loan investment strategy. This has a significantly positive impact on Main Street’s financial results, including $8.8 million contribution to net investment income in the third quarter of 2025 ($25.4 million contribution to net investment income in the nine months ended September 30, 2025, and $34.3 million contribution to net investment income in the year ended December 31, 2024). This is a diversified income stream adding differentiated degree of steadiness to the business.

The Big Risks

Of course, Main Street faces risks that investors should consider.

Non-accruals and default: For starters, Main Street is providing capital to riskier private companies in the lower middle market. The high coupon interest on the loans it provides (as we saw in an earlier graphic) demonstrates the higher risks and is elevated in attempt to compensate for taking on the risk. Nonetheless, default remains a risk, particularly if macroeconomic conditions worsen and multiple companies all default on their loans at the same time. Fortunately, nonaccruals improved to 1.2% of fair value (3.6% cost) in the most recent quarter (see table below), relatively low and somewhat expected.

Credit Spreads are another way (a proxy) to more-broadly measure risk and potential default among riskier loans, such as those made by Main Street. However, as you can see in the chart below, credit spreads remain relatively low.

(Click here for the full market risk report.)

And according to Main Street CFO, Ryan Nelson, (during the most recent quarterly call), the company remains relatively well-positioned in this regard:

“Our total investment income for the third quarter was $139.8 million, increasing by $3 million or 2.2% over the third quarter of 2024 and decreasing by $4.1 million or 2.9% from the second quarter of 2025. Interest income decreased by $7.3 million from a year ago and increased by $2.4 million from the second quarter of 2025. The decrease from prior year was principally attributable to a decrease in interest rates, primarily resulting from decreases in benchmark index rates on our floating rate debt investments and decreases in interest rate spreads on existing debt investments and an increase in investments on nonaccrual status, partially offset by the impact of increased net investment activity.”

Interest Rates: Interest rate dynamics also pose a risk, as you can see in the table below. However, Main Street’s strong liquidity and lower leverage add a margin of safety against changing rates.

The Bottom Line

Despite the relatively lower yield (as compared to other BDCs) and the relatively higher price-to-book value (as compared to other BDCs), Main Street Capital is not an overvalued bubble. Quite the contrary, it’s a top-notch, differentiated BDC, offering a powerful growth trajectory in dividend and net asset value. Not only have Main Street’s investors easily outperformed other BDCs over the last nearly 20 years since its IPO (see earlier chart), but I expect Main Street to continue outperforming in the years ahead. If you are an income-focused investor, it’s absolutely worth considering for a spot in your prudently diversified, long-term portfolio. I own it in mine.

data.title: Fiserv: The Guidance Cut Masks A Turnaround Opportunity
data.description:
Investment thesis

Fiserv (FISV) is a global payment and fintech company that has reset its expectations in the guidance provided in the Q3 2025 earnings release. The market has penalized the stock in a pretty aggressive way (Figure 1), lowering the stock price from $126.17 per share to the current $60.84. The market anticipated problems with the company, and the stock traded at $235.69 in March 2025. I consider this dip excessive, and my valuation concludes the company is worth at least $112.1 per share, an 84.4% premium over its stock price. With conservative assumptions, I value management’s audacity to change the direction of the company to focus more on the customer and platform development. I appreciate the current position of Fiserv in the market and the Clover platform as essential assets for a turnaround.

Execution risks are relevant in a market with pressure from emerging software companies that remains quite competitive, and a successful turnaround is not assured. Even with those considerations, my estimation of the probability of a value higher than the current stock price is 85.75%.

Shifting expectations

Guidance has been lowered from a revenue growth rate of 10% to 12% to a range of 3.5% to 4.0% in organic revenue growth. The reasons for the right-sizing of its forecast are that there won’t be any compensation for the decreasing revenues in Argentina, the short-term cost reduction that could impact clients won’t be executed, and, in general, the recalibration of the original assumptions.

It is always hard to lower expectations in a Fintech business where you are supposed to transform the financials of retail businesses. However, as an analyst, those are special moments where you could find opportunities. I think Fiserv’s CEO, Michael Lyons, has been brave to announce lower guidance, and this change in expectations is definitely a positive for Fiserv's investment thesis.

Besides this character, I like the company’s priority for the client and stopping tactical decisions designed to boost margin results at the expense of the customer. And I also appreciate the assets that the company has in the market, such as Clover, Commerce Hub, Finxact, STAR, Accel, Optis, and Vision Next. Those platforms and software are in a good position in the market, as we will see in the next session.

A promising market position

Despite those expectations, what lies beneath is a solid business in the market, and that is what we should lean on. The company is one of the largest companies in the industry at $5.2 billion in revenue (Figure 3), just behind Block’s revenue at $6.1 billion and way larger than the niche-specific Toast (TOST). That means the current customer base has trusted Fiserv, even if the last actions were non-customer-friendly. Besides, the company is aiming to push growth. Clover has been introduced in large international markets as Brazil and Europe. And the company is moving up-market to reach big retailers with Commerce Hub.

Fiserv's gross margin is one of the highest in the market at 59%, better than Block’s 44% and Toast’s 26% (Figure 4). It is important to notice that it is a good base to leverage into a business that is becoming more subscription-based, with higher fixed costs. Fiserv has the economics to invest in platform development to stay competitive and reach a leadership position in a competitive fintech market.

As always, I use Cash margin, net income adjusted for non-cash items such as amortization and depreciation, stock-based compensation, and deferred income tax, offering a clearer view of operational cash generation than standard margin metrics, as the main margin indicator. In Figure 5, you can see that Cash margin has lowered from 32% to 25% in line with what Fiserv’s CEO has stated that they won’t “cut certain costs, improve margins in the short term, but are now limiting our ability to serve clients in a world-class way.” I consider the company can stay at those lower margins if they invest in product and experiences for their customers.

At the end, Fiserv is the company that better converts each revenue into free cash flow, with a 50% free cash flow-to-revenue compared to 23% in Block and 9% in Toast. This is a good baseline for a business whose management is focused on the customer, the main value driver for the future of a business.

Valuation

In my valuation methodology, I discount projected free cash flows at an appropriate WACC. I will estimate the value drivers in Figure 7 in my ten-year model.

For revenue, I estimate that the company is going to decrease by 6% in 2025 and it will start growing in 2026 at a modest 2%. Later, the company will increase the growth rate to 5% in 2030 and stay at this growth rate until 2025. I consider, in a conservative approximation, that the maximum growth rate that the company is going to reach is 5% and considering the year 2026 as a year to make all the adjustments necessary and a transition year.

I estimate a ratio of cash margin-to-revenue that decreases to 20% in 2027 after 2026 management makes changes in the operation, and they are not able to compete with a higher margin, especially due to higher research and development costs. I see no big changes in the net working capital and capital expenditure.

Cash flows will be discounted at 7.4% WACC because the beta is 1.2, which I have estimated, because the historical measurement is less relevant due to its new situation. The risk-free rate is 4.2%. Debt, as a percentage of market capitalization, is 30%, and I consider it to have no excess cash. The perpetual growth rate is set at 3%, which is slightly better aligned with the long-term GDP growth rate.

My value estimate is $112.1 per share (Figure 8), up 84.4% from its current stock price. As we have seen in Figure 8, Fiserv’s Forward P/E comes down to 7 from a high close to 25 at the beginning of the year. The market was anticipating problems with the company. The last reset in expectations, the multiple went down from 17 to 7.

Risks

The first risk Fiserv is facing is the capacity to execute in an industry where high-tech players are emerging and dominating the market, such as Block and Toast. I see difficulties in moving fast in the software world. First Data acquisition has posed large liabilities in the culture and capacities of the workforce. And platform integration is key for customer satisfaction and to compete face-to-face with Block and Toast. The main software, Clover, was growing at 15-20% historically, and in the last earnings release, management stated an 8% GPV growth rate.

Margin deterioration can extend further in the coming quarters due to this competitive environment if the company needs to incur more expenses in research and development. EBITDA margin was 45.8% in Q3 2024 and has been reduced to 36.8% in Q3 2025.

We have to consider two threats that are relevant to Fiserv’s operations. Argentina is still a source of high currency and inflation risk. Leverage, debt-to-capital, has increased from 41% in Q3 2022 to 55% in Q3 2025. I do not view this as critical at present because operating cash flows to interest expense is a manageable 4.3, but it warrants close monitoring.

Risks are controlled in my estimation. In very aggressive assumptions, the probability of getting a value greater than the current stock price is controlled. I modeled in my risk simulation software, Crystall Ball, a variation in revenue growth from +/-5% in 95% of the cases. This means that the company won’t grow at all in my model in the most pessimistic scenario. In the case of cash margin, this variation in 95% of the cases goes from -5 percentage points to 5 percentage points. This means that in the worst case, the company will decrease from 25% to 15% in 2027. With those inputs, the model gives me a probability of a value greater than the current stock price of 85.75%.

Conclusion

In my opinion, the stock is undervalued after management changed guidance in Q3 2025. Forward P/E has decreased to a cheap value of 7, and this situation is a typical “buy the dip” situation vs. “a value trap”. I am confident the company is going to fix the culture and processes to transform Fiserv into a competitive player in the fintech space. The company can take advantage of great assets and platforms and a good market position.

I value the company at $112.1 per share, an 84.4% premium over its current stock price and a probability to exceed the current stock price at 85.75%. I recommend buying the company.

data.title: Western Midstream: Combining Best‑In‑Class 9.3% Yield And Stability
data.description:
Intro

I believe, when an investor looks at Western Midstream Partners, LP (WES), the first thing that catches the eye is its attractively high dividend yield — nearly 9.5%. In most cases, such a yield signals that something is not well beneath the surface or that there is a clear, visible reason why investors prefer to avoid it.

Continuing this line of thought, the best examples that first come to mind are tobacco companies. While today they have largely diversified beyond being purely old-style tobacco businesses—thereby gaining broader acceptance among many investor groups, which in turn has driven their yields down—in the past, they were a group of companies that consistently carried yields of around 10% or even higher.

Still, for a large segment of investors, they were mostly a "no-go," whether for ethical reasons or other considerations, and that was the main reason why their dividend yields remained so high. This occurred despite the fact that their dividends were relatively sustainable and well-backed by strong finances.

When it comes to WES, or other similarly structured MLPs in general, if we exclude the possibility of hidden risks in the company's operations (which is arguably not the case here), one might conclude it’s not a "no-go" type of investment, but rather a "wait a minute" case. The reasoning is that MLPs have a structure quite different from ordinary stocks or ETFs, and that structural difference alone can cause hesitation and make some investors cautious.

The first sign of differentiation lies in the terminology: you don’t buy shares, you buy units. Once you purchase a unit, you become a unitholder. And unitholders don’t receive dividends; they receive distributions. In other words, we’re not dealing with a “better to avoid” sector, but rather with a so‑called "different kind of animal."

Seasoned investors familiar with these "different animals" — MLPs — know well that while they are structured differently from traditional exchange‑traded instruments, they can be highly compelling investments, particularly for those focused on income. The key is to develop a clear understanding of the specific details of how and why they are treated differently, and to dig deeper into the tax nuances that accompany their unique rules.

Depending on an investor’s horizon and strategy, these tax considerations can provide an additional advantage if smartly applied when evaluating investments in WES or similar MLPs.

Attractiveness and Sustainability of WES’s Yield

I believe that the complexity of MLP structures may explain why WES and MLPs generally offer yields that, if observed in ordinary stocks or ETFs, would typically be interpreted as a sign of questionable sustainability.

The following presentation illustrates well the attractiveness of WES’s yield compared to alternatives:

In addition, the next chart shows how WES’s income is segmented — natural gas, widely recognized as a key component in the secular growth of electricity generation, is the primary source of income. Approximately 60% of total EBITDA is derived from fees related to natural gas:

Another slide provides an overview of why WES’s dividend yield, in my view, could be considered stable — its cash flows are driven not by the often volatile oil and natural gas prices, but by the fees generated from their transportation.

Yet another support for the yield’s relative stability thesis is that the contracts are secured on a long‑term basis:

Rating Considerations

After completing this analysis, I rate WES as a Buy. Western Midstream fits well within income‑focused investor portfolios, and I did not find any factors that would suggest excessive risks or threats to the underlying yield. On the contrary, the business appears stable, distributions are well covered, and management’s growth strategies seem both reasonable and solid.

Western Midstream in Brief

WES Story

Western Midstream traces its roots back to August 21, 2007, when Western Gas Partners, LP was formed by Anadarko Petroleum Corporation. In May 2008, Western Gas Partners, LP began trading on the NYSE as a master limited partnership (MLP) under the ticker symbol WES.

Between 2009 and 2019, when WES was a subsidiary of Anadarko, the parent contributed various assets and subsidiary ownership interests to WES, many of which remain part of its organizational structure today. After Occidental (OXY) acquired Anadarko in 2019, WES became OXY's subsidiary. In 2020, WES was transitioned into a standalone organization.

Today, WES employs more than 1,200 people and is headquartered in The Woodlands, Houston, Texas.

Ownership Structure:

Explaining MLPs:

From Energy Infrastructure Council:

A master limited partnership (MLP) is a limited partnership whose interests (known as “units”) are traded on public exchanges, just like corporate stock. MLPs engage in active businesses, primarily in the energy industry.

A limited partnership has one or more general partners (i.e. individuals, corporations, or other partnerships) who manage the partnership; it also has limited partners, who provide capital to the partnership but have no role in its management. When an investor buys units in an MLP, he or she becomes a limited partner.

Why Choose An MLP?

From VettaFi:

An MLP has the liquidity of a publicly traded company as well as an advantageous tax structure that allows MLPs, like all partnerships, to pay no federal taxes at the company level. This tax structure is one reason that MLPs can pay out distributions noticeably higher than those of traditional C-Corporations.

Tax Benefits

From KPMG:

As a very general matter, the income of publicly traded corporations is taxed twice—once at the corporate level (when earned) and again at the shareholder level (when distributed).By contrast, a PTP (Publicly traded partnerships) – also MLPs (author) that is classified as a partnership is a “passthrough” entity. For U.S. federal income tax purposes, its income is not taxed at the entity level but rather flows through to its partners (on a Schedule K-1) and is taxed at the partner level. Because such a PTP is not subject to entity-level tax, it may have more cash available (relative to a similarly situated publicly traded corporation) for acquiring assets and businesses, which can be advantageous in negotiating acquisitions and securing debt financing. It also may have more cash available (relative to a similarly situated corporation) for making distributions to public investors, which can contribute to a premium valuation for its units.

In summary, trading and receiving payouts work much like ordinary stocks or ETFs. The key difference lies in taxation: payouts are treated as distributions to partners (and ownership of even a single MLP unit makes one a partner), which are taxed differently. The following table illustrates these mechanics well:

Not all MLPs are K‑1 eligible, and this needs to be clarified before investing in one. WES is K‑1 eligible, meaning investors receive a Schedule K‑1 once a year. This form reports transactions such as distributions, unit purchases and sales, and depreciation, and reconciles balances accordingly.

WES Business Overview

General explanation of “Midstream” from VettaFi:

MLPs own the pipelines, storage tanks, and processing facilities that bring energy from the wellhead to America’s doorstep and increasingly to the coast for exports. In the energy industry, these activities describe “midstream,” which is the bridge between production (upstream) and consumption (downstream).

WES In Figures:

Recent Developments

Q3 Earnings

The company reported Q3 GAAP EPS of $0.87, in line with expectations. Revenue came in at $952.5 million, up 7.8% year‑over‑year, but fell short of estimates by $12 million.

Company Highlights Q3:

Outlook

WES expects results to be toward the upper end of its 2025 guidance ranges, with Adjusted EBITDA projected between $2.35 billion and $2.55 billion and capital expenditures between $625 million and $775 million. Additionally, WES anticipates generating Free Cash Flow above the high end of its 2025 guidance range of $1.28 billion to $1.48 billion.

Distributions

As seen below, distributions are well covered by cash flow:

According to the company’s projections, the current capital allocation strategy is expected to be maintained, with distributions modestly expanding over time:

Reasoning Behind the Recent Acquisition of Aris Water Solutions

Why?

During the Q3 earnings call, CEO Oscar Brown shared more insights into the Aris acquisition:

Combined with the newly acquired Aris assets and team, WES is now a midstream leader in Texas and New Mexico for produced water gathering, transportation, disposal, recycling and beneficial reuse.

..anybody can probably build gas lines since in New Mexico, if you've got sort of the footprint and the capabilities, but solving the water piece is becoming sort of a critical issue and a threshold issue for development. So we feel like that gives us some leverage in the marketplace and sort of the organic growth to New Mexico

Valuation

For WES, the yield and the degree to which cash flow covers distributions are key factors to watch in my view. Both are currently in strong shape. Moreover, relative valuation versus the sector median suggests undervaluation, while WES’s own historical comparison indicates a slightly higher valuation than its long‑term average. Overall, to me, the valuation appears attractive for income‑oriented investors.

Risks

In my view, a key risk is WES’s dependence on Occidental (OXY). Because Occidental accounts for the majority of WES’s business flows, being the largest partner at the same time, this relationship represents both a risk and a benefit. Having one partner account for most flows is always a risk worth highlighting, though at present I do not consider it a material threat.

While WES does not have direct sensitivity to oil and natural gas prices, it does have indirect exposure—if prices fall, some wells may be shut in, and pipeline volumes could decline.

Macro risks are also relevant: although WES business could be well capable of withstanding a modest downturn, a deeper recession would likely affect all commodity flows as well.

Takeaway

In my view, Western Midstream is an attractive investment for income‑oriented investors. The core business is stable and solid, with assets primarily tied to natural gas, which is well correlated with rising AI-related electricity demand. Growth initiatives appear reasonable, capital allocation plans are balanced, and distributions are well covered and expected to grow.

Management has demonstrated strong execution, and the company’s financial position remains sound. The distribution yield is best‑in‑class, with limited risk to payouts over the foreseeable future, in my view. That said, investors should carefully consider the specific tax treatment of MLPs and assess how it aligns with their personal investment strategy. Overall, I believe WES is a solid addition to income‑focused portfolios.

data.title: PALL: Everything You Need To Know About The Palladium ETF
data.description:
The abrdn Physical Palladium Shares ETF (NYSEARCA:PALL) is in focus as we unpack this palladium tracker fund's key attributes. Our content usually includes directional opinions. However, this piece forms part of our educational series, which addresses overlooked nuances of popular ETFs, allowing investors to make more informed decisions.

Without further ado, here's everything you need to know about the abrdn Physical Palladium Shares ETF.

The Methodology of the abrdn Physical Palladium Shares ETF

This ETF is relatively simple by design. It uses its investor inflows to acquire physical palladium, secured in a London vault and inspected bi-annually by Bureau Veritas Commodities UK Limited. As a result, PALL reflects the palladium spot price, less expenses and market frictions.

Figure 1 illustrates a few key talking points. Notably, it shows that Bank of New York Mellon is the trustee, a total expense ratio of 0.60% and Net Assets of $622,116,604.20 @ $1,235/oz (per September 30th, 2025). In addition, it should be made clear that PALL doesn't distribute dividends, it is a price return asset.

Aberdeen Investments' reported returns since-inception of 6.35%. Annualized observations show a degree of cyclicality, suggesting suitability to market timers. Alternatively, PALL could be looked at as a diversification vehicle, affirmed by the fact that its correlation with the S&P 500 is tame (reported at 0.123).

Key Drivers

Industrial Cycles

Much of the PALL's cyclicality can be explained by the real economy. Industrial demand has long-run influencers such as changes in demographic, technological development, and education levels. However, cyclically, variables such as monetary policy, economic shock events, and consumers' fluctuating utility influence industrial output, and as a consequence, the demand for industrial metals such as palladium.

It's worth noting that approximately 80% of palladium is used for catalytic converters, making ICE vehicle demand central. The emergence of Electric Vehicles has altered the long-run demand outlook for palladium, but in short-run cycles, interest rates, consumer confidence, and money supply will likely dictate proceedings.

Various metrics can be tracked to judge the industrial cycle. I've mentioned the importance of interest rates and consumer demand on a few occasions. However, Manufacturing PMIs also tell a story – notice PALL's correlation with the US ISM Manufacturing PMI, whenever large shifts have occurred.

Supply

Approximately 75% of the world's palladium is supplied by Russia and South Africa. The concentrated supply in two geopolitically sensitive countries adds uncertainty. Russia's embargoes may spill over into broad-based trade. Moreover, South Africa's frictions with the Trump administration and local infrastructure concerns add further pricing points.

Sources that cover supply outlook include, the World Platinum Investment Council. Additionally investors could make use of company Capital Expenditure plans. For example, tracking Impala Platinum (OTCQX:IMPUY) or Valterra Platinum's (OTCPK:ANGPY) management commentary would add color to the core analysis.

Side Note: Platinum and Palladium are usually mined in tandem through 2+ element mines, instead of on a standalone basis.

PALL Portfolio Diversification Benefits, Futures, and Hedging

Financial market-based variables will alter PALL's price throughout time. As previously mentioned, PALL has tabled a negligible correlation to the S&P 500 since its inception, illustrating its diversification properties. In addition, fiscal risk premiums have spiked in developed nations since the COVID-19 pandemic, providing reason for investors to park capital in alternatives (as a means of protecting against currency risk). Palladium might provide a viable alternative to commodities such as Gold and Silver.

Furthermore, palladium prices are influenced by the futures market. Arbitrageurs will often act on dislocated discrepancies between spot and future prices. For example, if the curve is in steep contango, we could see traders pushing spot prices up by buying physical palladium while shorting futures contracts.

Risk Factors

To illustrate the worst-case risk of owning PALL, I highlight its max drawdown of ~73.63%, which exceeds that of the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY). Moreover, the vehicle's 30-day rolling volatility of 49.26% is considerably higher than the SPY's of 13.84%. Commodities often carry higher volatility than stock indices; nonetheless, a near 50%, 30-day rolling volatility figure should arguably not be taken lightly.

Here are a few Qualitative risks worth considering.

  • Structural Changes Amid EV – As mentioned earlier, the emergence of electric vehicles has disrupted the natural progression of palladium demand. The most problematic concerns relates to the uncertainty more than the actual outcome of EV's eventual use case. Higher uncertainty will likely translate into a higher volatility regime.
  • Market Liquidity Events – Asymmetrical demand can enter the fray whenever market turmoil occurs, exacerbating commodity markets. The impact was illustrated in 2020, when PALL's 30-day trailing volatility shot up above 100%.
  • Operational Risk – PALL deals with high value physical commodities. A large degree of operational prowess is required to transfer the commodities to and from vaults and to ensure safekeeping, validity, and trustee responsibilities. Whether the market has priced an operational risk premium remains unclear, yet it remains a possibility.

Portfolio Suitability

Market Timers

Factors such as PALL's volatility, cyclicality, and relative liquidity makes it a prime candidate for market timing portfolios. A well-constructed trading model or a clear read on the market could certainly see PALL be used as an alpha generator.

Risk-Averse Diversifiers

Risk-averse portfolios may benefit from PALL as a diversification tool amid heightened sovereign risks and fears of an "AI bubble" that could derail the stock market. Holding PALL throughout the cycle may not present telling returns; however, it could act as a store of wealth for a well-diversified portfolio.

Conclusion

The abrdn Physical Palladium Shares ETF, otherwise known as PALL, provides investors with liquid access to physical palladium. Historical returns have shown low correlation to the S&P 500 accompanied by significant cyclical swings, with the latter being a consequence of the industrial cycle, concentrated global supply, and market liquidity events.

Market timing portfolios and sovereign risk diversifiers would most likely deem PALL suitable. Despite the ETF's high volatility, threats posed by EV, and operational considerations, PALL could function as a nimble tool to alpha-seeking portfolios.

This article answers these three main questions about PALL:

  • How correlated is PALL to stock market returns?
  • Does PALL hold physical palladium?
  • Can PALL help diversify an investor's portfolio?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: STAG Industrial Q3: Earnings Beat, Consistent Monthly Income
data.description:
I rate STAG Industrial, Inc. (STAG) a Buy, for income focused investors interested in real estate investment trusts (REITs). STAG owns and manages industrial warehouse space, of which 31% is used for the growing e-commerce industry. Recently posted Q3 results were very positive, showing strong momentum on all key metrics. Funds from operations (FFO) increased 8.3% from a year ago. Occupancy remained at 96.8% for the operating portfolio.

STAG is a high-quality investment with a long history of generating consistent income paid monthly. For those interested in reinvesting the dividend, the REIT outperforms most real estate benchmark indexes.

The most important factors that influence my opinion of the REIT are:

  • Proven business model in industrial real estate.
  • Growth opportunities from internal sources such as rent escalators, external by acquisitions and development.
  • Pays a consistent, steadily increasing, monthly dividend yielding 3.8%.
  • The next dividend of $0.1242 has an ex-dividend date of 11/28 and 12/31/25.
  • Beat earnings estimates for the last 4 quarters.
  • Q3 beats earnings estimates, setting up positive momentum going into 2026.

I previously covered the REIT in May, with the article “STAG Industrial: Consistent Dividend Income From A Quality REIT,” where I rated it a Buy. Since then, it is up 10.4%.

Seeking Alpha Quant rates the stock a Strong Buy 4.64 (as of 11/26/2025), which supports my rating. Note the Quant rating is highest in Profitability and Momentum, which speaks to the proven business model, in my view.

Industrial Warehouse Property REIT

Headquartered in Boston, STAG Industrial REIT is focused on the acquisition, ownership, and operation of industrial properties throughout the United States. It went public in 2011 and is a member of the S&P MidCap 400.

STAG currently owns 601 buildings, with 119.2 million square feet, over 41 states. It has a Fitch Credit Rating of BBB, and Moody’s Baa2, Stable.

Q3 2025 Earnings: Another Beat

On October 29, the company declared Q3 earnings, announcing the following financial highlights through September 30, 2025:

  • Net income of $0.26 per share, up +13%.
  • Core Funds from Operations (FFO) of $0.66 per share, up +10%.
  • Cash Net Operating Income of $162.3 million, up +9.3%.
  • Acquired 2 buildings consisting of 986,410 square feet for $101.5 million.
  • Acquired one vacant land parcel for $2.9 million.
  • Sold 3 buildings consisting of 588,591square feet for $82.2 million.
  • Occupancy rate of 95.8% on the total portfolio, 96.8% on the Operating Portfolio

Overall, the quarterly results continued to impress. STAG achieved positive growth on every key metric including income, earnings, and occupancy rates. Given the type of business that is industrial warehousing, an investor doesn’t expect much volatility. The growth in key metrics proves the business model is operating well, such as building and selling properties, managing tenants, rent increases etc.

Continued Outperformance

A $10,000 investment in STAG over the past 10 years, with dividends invested, tripled in value to $31,020. This performance beat both the Real Estate Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLRE) and the Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ) by over $10,000.

Valuation

STAG has a price to FFO of 18.49, which is a 20% premium to the sector at 15.43. This doesn’t concern me as leaders in the industry usually trade at a premium to the sector because they are in a better position to succeed than the laggards.

The company has a total debt to capital ratio of 47.27% which is below the sector of 49.44%, which is seen as a good sign. The takeaway is STAG is performing while taking less risk.

On the dividend front, STAG has a forward dividend yield of 3.8% vs the sector at 4.81%. Again, this speaks more to the demand investors have for the shares, knowing it’s a consistent, quality dividend.

The chart below shows the consistent, ever-rising dividend STAG has been paying over the past 10 years.

The chart below shows the annual distribution policy showing consistent income. Notice the dividend has been raised each year since 2012. So too has the yield dropped as investors are buying and holding the REIT due to its consistent income generation. In my view, this shows the REIT has a proven business model in a stable and growing industry.

Competition

In a comparison to peers, STAG has one of the highest dividend yields at 3.8%, but the lowest 5-year dividend growth rate at 0.7%. It also has the highest debt to equity ratio at 89.6%, with over $3 billion in debt. However, for industrial real estate properties, this is fairly common in my experience.

Overall, the peers are fairly similar. The dividend yields are about 3%, the 10-year returns are all over 200%, and even the market caps are similar.

Prologis, Inc. (PLD) – as one of the largest industrial REITs with $121 billion in market capitalization, PLD has an average dividend yield for the sector of 3.2%, and the second to the highest 10-year total return at 297.5%.

Rexford Industrial Realty, Inc. (REXR) – offers the highest dividend yield of the group at 4.2%, the highest 5-year dividend growth rate of 15.5%, however one of the lowest 5-year total returns at -0.7%.

EastGroup Properties, Inc. (EGP) – has the highest 10-year total return at 314%, combined with one of the lowest debts to equity ratios, making it fairly attractive in my view.

First Industrial Realty Trust Inc. (FR) – has the lowest dividend yield at 3.1%, with a good 10-year total return of 233%.

Terreno Realty Corp (TRNO) – has an average yield and total return.

Outlook

STAG is in a good position to continue their strong momentum into 2026. Potential rate cuts in December or January will lower the cost of debt, which can reduce cost and increase profitability. There are few signs of a recession, so consumption is expected to continue, which will keep the warehouses full.

Q4 2025 will report February 10, 2026.

Risks To Rating

The largest risk to STAG is rising interest rates for an organization that uses a great deal of debt financing. While it isn’t likely that rates will rise, this risk should be observed going forward.

The other major risk is macroeconomic forces causing a recession. During a consumer slowdown, which could put pressure on companies to reduce inventories. Less inventories means less demand for industrial warehouse space.

Bottom Line

I rate STAG Industrial as a Buy for income investors interested in consistent rising monthly income from real estate investments trusts (REITs). This industrial REIT has a proven strategy that is working overtime. They develop some buildings to add to the portfolio, acquire others, and then sell off assets that are no longer a good fit. STAG has a high occupancy rate of 96.8% suggesting that management is doing the right things to keep tenants satisfied and resigning the lease.

Q3 earnings beat were the best proof of STAG’s ability. For the first quarter, STAG grew on all key metrics including income, earnings and kept a high occupancy rate. This performance gives comfort to dividend investors who rely on STAG’s consistent, steadily increasing, monthly dividend, currently paying 3.8% annually. While growth has slowed, data suggests the REIT is a consistent source of dividend income.

data.title: VCR: Benefits And Risk Of This Consumer Discretionary ETF
data.description:
Fast Facts

Vanguard Consumer Discretionary ETF (VCR) was launched on 01/26/2004 and tracks the MSCI USA IMI Consumer Discretionary 25-50 Index. The strategy is also available as a mutual fund (VCDAX) by the same issuer. VCR has a portfolio of 292 stocks, a dividend yield of 0.75% and an expense ratio of 0.09%. Distributions are paid quarterly. It is a large and liquid ETF, with $7.1 billion of AUM (assets under management) and an average daily dollar volume of $14.5 million.

The sponsor Vanguard Group was founded in 1975, is based in Malvern, Pennsylvania, and has about $11 trillion in total AUM. Vanguard has a unique ownership structure: it is owned by its U.S.-based funds, which are owned by their shareholders.

Strategy

As described by MSCI, the underlying index includes large, mid and small cap companies listed on U.S. exchanges and classified in the Consumer Discretionary sector by the Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS®). This sector includes industries that tend to be sensitive to economic cycles: automotive, household durable goods, textiles and apparel, leisure equipment, hotels, restaurants, leisure facilities, media production and services, consumer retailing and services.

The index is weighted based on free float market capitalization and rebalanced on a quarterly basis. The “25-50” diversification rule ensures that no single issuer represents more than 25% of asset value, and all issuers weighting more than 5% individually don’t exceed 50% in aggregate. The turnover rate was 6% in the most recent fiscal year.

This article will use as a benchmark the Consumer Discretionary Select Sector Index, represented by the Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR® Fund (XLY).

Portfolio

About 64% of the fund's asset value is in large and mega-cap companies, while mid and small caps weigh 20% and 16%, respectively. The portfolio has a focus on two sub-industries: broadline retail (26.8%) and automobile manufacturing (21%). Other sub-industries are below 10%.

The portfolio is very concentrated, with high exposure in two companies. The top 10 issuers, listed in the next table, represent 61.9% of asset value, with Amazon and Tesla weighing 24% and 18.7%.

data.title: Seadrill: Disappointing Quarter But Improving Medium-Term Prospects
data.description:
Note:

Seadrill Limited or "Seadrill" (SDRL) has been covered by me previously, so investors should view this as an update to my earlier articles on the company.

Please note also that Seadrill is part of our exclusive coverage at Value Investor's Edge.

Earlier this month, leading offshore driller Seadrill Limited reported mixed third quarter results. While revenues outperformed muted expectations, mostly due to additional work for two rigs in the U.S. Gulf, the company reported a surprise loss.

According to statements made in the company's quarterly report on form 10-Q, economic utilization suffered from unplanned downtime for rigs working offshore Brazil.

However, it took an analyst question on the conference call for management to provide additional details:

So during the quarter, one of our rigs operating in Brazil experienced a downtime event caused by design-related equipment failure. This issue resulted in operational downtime and additional cost to rectify same and implement corrective measures.

In addition, profitability continued to be impacted by a recent unfavorable legal ruling related to the establishment of the company's Sonadrill joint venture in Angola back in 2018, as outlined in more detail in the 10-Q:

In March 2023, Seadrill was served with a claim from an individual (...). The Claimant alleged breach of contract and unjust enrichment damages of approximately $72 million related to an alleged failure by the Company to pay the Claimant a fee for services in arranging the Sonadrill joint venture dating back to 2018. (...)

On July 11, 2025, the High Court rendered judgment in favor of the Claimant. In October 2025, the High Court ruled on the first tranche of damages for the period prior to October 2024, including interest and legal fees. (...)

Seadrill presently estimates that its aggregate liability (...) is unlikely to exceed $61 million (...). In October 2025, Seadrill paid the first tranche of damages of approximately $43 million. (...)

In Q3, the company recognized $4 million in additional management contract expenses as a result of the adverse ruling.

After four consecutive quarters of negative free cash flow, Seadrill finally generated a small amount of cash in Q3.

The company ended the quarter with $428 million in cash and cash equivalents, as well as $625 million in funded debt. Total liquidity amounted to $587 million.

Seadrill did not buy back any shares during the quarter. Consequently, available capacity under the company's current $500 million share repurchase authorization remained at $208 million.

On a sequential basis, backlog declined by approximately 4% to 2.5 billion.

On the conference call, management provided updated full-year guidance with increased revenue expectations offset by slightly lower Adjusted EBITDA projections and higher capital expenditures:

Unfortunately, Seadrill faces substantial legal challenges.

In Brazil, the company continues to defend against a $79 million tax claim:

The initial court ruled in favor of Seadrill Brazil, but the appellate court reversed the lower court decision in September 2023 and ruled in favor of the tax authorities, assessing a tax and interest thereon of approximately $79 million.

We will vigorously defend our position and, in the first quarter of 2024, our appeal was admitted by the higher courts, but the ultimate timing and outcome of this litigation cannot be determined.

In January, Seadrill Brazil was served with a $213 million claim from Petrobras (PBR), the company's largest customer, related to legacy drillship contracts.

According to disclosures made in the 10-Q, Petrobras might alternatively claim up to $825 million in "compensatory damages" rather than the $213 million in "delay penalties" currently pursued. The overhang is likely to persist for the time being:

We dispute liability and do not believe any damages are due to Petrobras from us, Sete Brazil or any of its related companies in connection with the Sete Brazil Project as either delay penalties or compensatory damages.

Petrobras and Seadrill have agreed to participate in voluntary mediation, and Petrobras has committed to not exercise any set-off rights pending the outcome of the mediation.

While Petrobras initially indicated that the mediation could commence in the third quarter of 2025, no date has been set and mediation is unlikely to begin before year-end.

In February, the Oslo District Court awarded former related party SFL Corporation (SFL) $48 million in damages, interest and legal costs in conjunction with the redelivery of the semi-submersible rig Hercules in 2023.

While Seadrill has appealed the ruling, the company was required to provide a $57 million guarantee to the Norwegian Enforcement Authorities.

Similar to peers, Seadrill pointed to increased tendering activity as a basis for higher global fleet utilization and return of pricing power by late 2026 or early 2027 with regions like Africa and Asia leading the charge.

However, the company continues to face substantial near-term idle time for a number of rigs, which will impact Seadrill's financial performance in 2026.

While 2027 should benefit from improved fleet utilization and full contributions from higher-margin contracts offshore Brazil, the company's less-than-stellar execution remains a cause for concern.

In combination with Seadrill's legal issues, a discount to peers appears warranted. As a result, I have lowered my assigned EV/EBITDA multiple from 5.5x to 5.0x and used very conservative profitability assumptions for 2026 and 2027.

Despite a lack of oil price support, offshore drilling stocks have performed remarkably well in recent months, likely due to widespread expectations of improving industry conditions going into 2027:

The rally has reduced theoretical upside quite meaningfully:

However, with valuation now based on very conservative assumptions and considering more than 20% upside from current levels, I am reiterating my "Buy" rating on Seadrill Limited's common shares.

Please note that the company still has two 7th generation drillships working for Petrobras offshore Brazil on painfully low legacy rates. While Petrobras holds priced options for the drillship West Saturn, the drillship West Carina is a prime candidate for an award under a current tender for a four-year contract in Petrobras' Buzios field. Unfortunately, the decision appears to have been delayed until 2026.

In addition, the currently warm-stacked 6th generation drillship West Capella appears to be well suited for a long-term contract award under a tender issued by Thailand's PTT Exploration and Production Public Company Limited or "PTTEP."

Any new long-term contract award at sufficient rates is likely to benefit Seadrill's share price quite meaningfully.

Risk Factors

Despite a somewhat lower correlation in recent months, oil prices will continue to determine the direction of industry stocks. A further drop in oil prices could result in additional project delays and reduction of customer capex budgets.

Bottom Line

Seadrill reported disappointing third quarter results, with profitability impacted by unplanned downtime for rigs offshore Brazil and expenses in conjunction with an unfavorable litigation outcome.

In addition, the company continues to face a number of ongoing legal challenges, which could result in substantial cash outflows.

While 2027 should benefit from improved fleet utilization and full contributions from higher-margin contracts offshore Brazil, Seadrill's less-than-stellar execution and legal risks remain a cause for concern.

Consequently, I have lowered my assigned EV/EBITDA multiple and based my new $36 price target on very conservative 2027 profitability expectations.

Given more than 20% upside from current levels, I am reiterating my "Buy" rating on Seadrill Limited's common shares.
