data.title: Corning Remains Expensive, Even With Its Growth Plan Exceeding Expectations
data.description:
There seems to be a lot of hype at the moment when it comes to Corning Incorporated (GLW). Operationally, this is a fascinating company, with its business centered around a wide array of activities like glass science, ceramic science, and optical physics. Management is also working on significantly reinventing the business, leaning into key growth areas while looking for ways to cut costs. We have started to see some progress on this front, with revenue rising nicely and bottom-line results improving massively. The firm is actually ahead of schedule in terms of its expectations to significantly grow revenue by between $4 billion and $6 billion by 2026. But this does not necessarily mean that shares deserve upside to match.

Back in September of this year, I acknowledged that the company was doing a fantastic job. It had a great growth plan in place, and the future was looking brighter than perhaps ever. However, my argument to counter this bullish assessment was that shares of the business were already looking rather pricey. And even if we projected out into what would likely be the 2027 fiscal year, the stock looked lofty at best. Ultimately, this led me to rate it a Hold to reflect my view that the stock would be unlikely to outperform the broader market for the foreseeable future. But since then, the market has disagreed with me, pushing the stock up 8% while the S&P 500 (SP500) is up only 3.2%. I wouldn't go so far as to say that this outperformance justifies a downgrade to something more bearish. But I do think that investors are playing with fire here and that underperformance moving forward is becoming increasingly likely.

Taking Another Look At Corning

The only new data that we have regarding Corning covers the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. But honestly, that was a really great quarter for the business. Revenue amounted to $4.10 billion for that time. That happens to be an astounding 20.9% increase over the $3.39 billion the company reported a year earlier.

But that's not all. The firm went from a net loss of $117 million last year to a net profit of $430 million this year. We saw operating cash flow jump from $699 million to $784 million. And if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $605 million to $774 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the company expanded from $725 million to $934 million. All of these figures can be seen in the chart above.

In the chart above, you can see results for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time last year. Once again, we see performance across the board without exception. Growth itself is not surprising. But growth of this magnitude most certainly is. And as you will see shortly, a lot of this has to do with the company's operating strategy. But to understand that, we need to dig deeper into each of its operating segments.

Certainly, the main operating segment that investors need to be aware of is the Optical Communications segment. This is the part of the company that caters to the optical based communications infrastructure space in order to help facilitate video services, data and voice services, and communications, and it also involves the firm's enterprise network group that provides optical based communication networks that are sold to businesses, governments, and individuals. Ultimately, this boils down to the services that the firm provides for data centers and their need for optical fiber products like cable assemblies, closures, and the like.

In the chart above, you can see financial performance for this segment for the most recent quarter, as well As for the first nine months of this year. These figures are stacked up against what we see for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time of 2024. Revenue jumped massively, with the surge in the third quarter of 32.6% compared to what the company saw the previous year. This increase was, according to management, driven by strong demand for its generative AI products under the enterprise business, as well as in its carrier business for interconnect products and fiber to the home products as data centers continue to pop up everywhere. Naturally, this rise in revenue would bring with it a hike in profitability as well. Segment profits jumped from $175 million to $295 million. This is a testament to the strength of this unit.

This is not the only part of the company that has experienced growth. In fact, it had two other operating segments that did quite well during this time. The best of these, outside of the Optical Communications segment, was the Specialty Materials segment, which uses different formulations for glass, glass ceramics, and crystals, that are used in certain hardware like metrology instruments. The durable glass that this segment produces has historically been used for the back of iPhones. But it also provides different items for the semiconductor optics industry.

Revenue during the most recent quarter amounted to $621 million. That was up nicely from the $548 million reported a year earlier. Premium glass for mobile devices experienced a surge in demand. I wish management would have provided more detail here when they announced results for the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. The only thing they mentioned, in their investor call, was that the continued adoption of some of its innovations in this space supported "customers’ flagship product launches." Regardless, an overall increase in revenue was bound to bring with it a higher level of profitability as well. Year over year, profits grew from $72 million to $113 million.

The only other operating segment to see some growth during this time was the Automotive segment, which produces certain ceramic substrates and filter products that are used for the emissions control functionality in mobile applications. Some of these items that it has in its portfolio are also used for emission control systems, catalyzers, engines, and more. The rise in revenue here from $430 million to $454 million was fairly small. But this was because there seems to have been some weakness in key markets like Europe and North America when it came to light duty and heavy-duty vehicles. However, global passenger vehicle demand more broadly has been growing, with China being a rare bright spot.

The rise in demand for what management described as premium content also helped here. Management did not offer much in the way of detail on this front. But they did say that they benefited from additional content needed for vehicle emissions regulations to be met, and they also saw greater adoption when it came to technical glass and optics offerings. Overall, this rise in revenue did push profitability up from $51 million to $68 million.

This is not to say that there weren't weak spots for the business. The Life Sciences segment experienced a dip in sales from $244 million to $242 million. This change is hardly worth mentioning. And in fact, management did not even provide any description behind this driver. However, they did provide a bit of detail when it came to the Display segment, which is the second-largest operating segment and which provides glass substrates for flat panel displays that are used in televisions and computer monitors. This has been a bit of a pain point for the company this year, with management blaming that on foreign currency fluctuations that more than offset higher pricing.

There is another piece of the business as well, which is known as its Hemlock and Emerging Growth Businesses. This is essentially a catch-all for all of the operations that don't fit under its other segments. Management has singled out its Hemlock Semiconductor Group, which produces and sells high purity polysilicon products that are used in the electronics and solar power industries, as well as in the pharmaceutical technologies space. Year over year, this part of the company grew revenue from $250 million to $364 million. Higher demand for its solar businesses proved helpful here. But I do wonder the extent to which this is part of a new norm, and the extent to which it has to do with changes on the political side.

I know that there was a surge in buying solar panels in the U.S. out of fear of both tariffs and the expiration of the tax write off that residential property owners get to take. That is expected to expire at the end of this year. I would imagine that we will see some near term strength here since the commercial solar panel tax benefit is still in play, but it is required under the law that any projects under that category start construction by July 4th of next year and that they are ultimately put in place by December 31st of 2027.

When it comes to the rest of this year, things are looking up. Management previously estimated that earnings per share would range between $0.68 and $0.72 for the final quarter of 2025. At the midpoint, this would be enough net profits to bring total net income this year up to $1.66 billion. And if we project our results based on what we have seen already for the year, we would get adjusted operating cash flow of $3.05 billion and EBITDA of $3.61 billion.

Normally, at this point, I would see how pricey shares are. But I think something else needs to be done first. And this would be a check-up on the company's current plan to create even more value for shareholders moving forward. By leaning heavily into certain growth opportunities, namely those involving data centers and AI, management is hoping to grow annualized revenue from the final quarter of 2023 to the final quarter of next year by between $4 billion and $6 billion.

Management is benefiting from certain maneuvers that it's making, with one example being what it considers to be a generative AI network scale out opportunity that revolves around the world's first low loss optical fiber. As the largest producer of fiber, cable, and multi fiber connectors in the world, Corning is in a prime position to benefit here. And with this technology, the firm hopes to further support hyperscale AI customers as they build out their data centers.

The total scale up opportunity here is estimated to be worth between two and three times its existing enterprise network business. And when it comes to its generative AI data center interconnect offerings, it is working with other companies such as Microsoft (MSFT) in order to accelerate production of its Hollow Core Fiber technology in order to improve Azure’s Cloud and AI workloads. By the end of the decade, the firm expects to grow this business to $1 billion in annual revenue.

This is not to say that all of its opportunities are centered around AI. Management also seems very optimistic about the solar space. They continue to make progress when it comes to the higher end chip segment for semiconductors that are used specifically in solar panel technology. The firm has even gone so far as to activate idle assets to cater to its domestic solar polysilicon customers. And over the next five years, it has over 80% of capacity when it comes to polysilicon and wafer offerings already booked. Overall, the firm expects to turn this into a $2.5 billion revenue stream by 2028.

As revenue increases, the company also expects to grow profitability. And so far, we are looking at some great progress. In the image above, you can see that the firm is well above its operating margin plan as of the third quarter of 2025, and it is targeting even greater spread here for the final quarter of this year. This is all part of its Springboard plan that management has been enacting for quite some time now in order to make this a more valuable company for investors.

In my previous article, I detailed that likely net profits on an annualized basis by the final quarter of 2026 would be about $1.67 billion. Adjusted operating cash flow would be $3.31 billion. And EBITDA would be around $4.17 billion. This is great in and of itself. But as I mentioned already, with the firm already well ahead of its operating margin ambitions, we could see these numbers come in higher. But unless they come in drastically higher, shares are still rather pricey.

In the chart above, you can see how the company is valued using results from 2024 and estimates for 2025. You can also see what it's valued at using current estimates for the final quarter of 2026. Honestly, these are rather lofty multiples, not only on an absolute basis, but relative to other similar firms as well.

In the table below, I stacked it up against five such businesses. While the stock is cheaper than three of these five firms on a price to earnings basis, it is more expensive than four of them when it comes to the other two approaches to valuing it. This definitely makes it a rather risky proposition for those who are value oriented like myself, even for those who provide additional wiggle room for high-quality industry leaders.

Company Price/Earnings Price/Operating Cash Flow EV/EBITDA
Corning 42.7 23.3 21.6
TDK Corporation (OTCPK:TTDKY) 26.3 10.6 10.7
Kyocera Corporation (OTCPK:KYOCY) 62.8 13.3 13.5
Coherent Corp (COHR) 224.3 48.9 24.2
Littelfuse (LFUS) 49.0 14.1 13.6
OMRON Corporation (OTCPK:OMRNY) 25.9 12.2 11.0

Takeaway

Based on the data provided, I believe that while Corning remains a stellar opportunity, there are better ways to make money in this market. The company is growing nicely, and it very well could even exceed expectations over the next couple of years. But at the same time, the stock is pricey, and with broader economic conditions worsening, I believe that a more cautious approach is warranted here. Because of this, I believe that maintaining a Hold rating is the right choice.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: CMS Slashes Prices Of 15 Major Drugs - Here's Why Pharma Valuations Won't Suffer
data.description:
Investment Overview - Background To Yesterday's Drug Price Cut Announcement

Earlier this month, in a note for Seeking Alpha, I covered the "historic" drug pricing agreements made between the world's most valuable Pharma company, Eli Lilly (LLY), and Danish Pharma giant Novo Nordisk (NVO), with the US government.

The deals involved two of the world's best-selling prescription drugs - Lilly's tirzepatide and Novo Nordisk's semaglutide, which are both indicated to treat patients with Obesity, and to treat patients with Type 2 Diabetes ("T2D"), branded respectively as Zepbound / Mounjaro and Wegovy / Ozempic - combined revenues in 2025 to the end of Q3 of ~$48bn.

These deals were made in response to President Trump's introduction of a "Most Favored Nation" ("MFN") pricing policy in the U.S. - essentially, Americans should pay no more for their prescription drugs that whichever nation charges the least.

But President Trump and his Republican government are by no means the first administration to challenge "Big Pharma" drug pricing policies - in 2022, Democrat President Biden signed the Inflation Reduction Act into law, which, amongst other things, gave the Department for Health and Human Services ("HHS") the power - for the first time - to intervene in drug pricing policy.

Last year, the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services ("CMS") were able to negotiate down prices of ten of the best-selling drugs in the U.S. The price of Pfizer's (PFE) blood thinner Eliquis, for example, which earned >$13bn of revenues in 2024, was cut from its list price of $521 for a 30-day supply, to $231, with the cut taking effect in 2026. Eliquis earned >$7bn in the first half of 2026.

Here are the other nine drugs affected by last-year's negotiations:

  • Xarelto - blood thinner, marketed and sold by Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) 2024 revenues $2.4bn - price cut (list price, for 30-day supply) from $517 - $197.
  • Januvia - diabetes, Merck (MRK), 2024 revenues $1.3bn - price cut from $527, to $113.
  • Jardiance, diabetes, Eli Lilly, 2024 revenues $3.34bn - price cut from $573, to $197.
  • Enbrel, rheumatoid arthritis, Amgen (AMGN), 2024 revenues $690m - price cut from $7.1k, to $2.35k.
  • Imbruvica, blood cancers, AbbVie (ABBV) / Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), 2024 revenues $3.4bn - price cut from $14.9k, to $9.3k.
  • Farxiga, diabetes / heart failure, AstraZeneca (AZN), 2024 revenues $7.7bn - price cut from $556, to $178.
  • Entresto, heart failure, Novartis (NVS), 2024 revenues $7.8bn - price cut from $628, to $295.
  • Stelara, psoriasis, Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), 2024 revenues $10.3bn - price cut from $13.8k, to $4.7k.
  • Fiasp / NovoLog, diabetes, Novo Nordisk (NVO), 2024 revenues $2.3bn - price cut from $495 - $119.

15 More Major Drug Prices Cut Yesterday - Wegovy's For Second Time This Month!

As we can see above, some of the price cuts imposed by CMS were severe - usually >50% - although as you can imagine, Pharmas were critical of the cuts, and the policy.

Patients rarely pay the full list price for drugs, they point out, as rebates are negotiated through Pharmacy Benefit Managers ("PBMs") who act on behalf on behalf of health insurance plans. According to Phrma, the pharmaceutical trade group, price controls stifle innovation and R&D, and may deny patients access to drugs that will be cut from insurer's coverage lists.

It is a brutal debate, but the CMS has not backed down, announcing 15 more drugs whose prices will be cut, from 2027, yesterday. Here's the full list:

  • Trelegy Ellipta - asthma, GSK (GSK), 2024 revenues $2.7bn - price cut from $654, to $175.
  • Xtandi - prostate cancer, Pfizer (PFE), 2024 revenues $2.1bn - price cut from $13.5k, to $7k.
  • Pomalyst - chemotherapy, Bristol Myers Squibb (BMY), 2024 revenues $3.55bn - price cut from $21.7k, to $8.65k.
  • Ofev - idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis, Boehringer Ingelheim, 2024 revenues ~$4bn - price cut from $12.6k, to $6.4k.
  • Ibrance - breast cancer, Pfizer (PFE), 2024 revenues ~$4.4bn - price cut from $15.7k, to $7.9k.
  • Linzess - Chronic Constipation, AbbVie (ABBV), 2024 revenues ~$4.4bn - price cut from $15.7k, to $7.9k.
  • Calquence - cancer med, AstraZeneca (AZN), 2024 revenues ~$3.1bn - price cut from $14.2k, to $8.6k.
  • Austedo and Austedo XR - Huntington's Disease, Teva Pharmaceuticals (TEVA), 2024 revenues ~1.6bnbn - price cut from $6.6k, to $4k.
  • Breo Ellipta - Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease ("COPD"), GSK, 2024 revenues ~$1.1bn - price cut from $397, to $67.
  • Xifaxan - diarrhea, Bausch Health (BHC), 2024 revenues ~2bn - price cut from $2.7k, to $1k.
  • Vraylar - antipsychotic, AbbVie (ABBV), 2024 revenues ~3.3bn - price cut from $1.4k, to $770.
  • Tradjenta - diabetes, Eli Lilly (LLY), 2024 revenues ~354m - price cut from $488, to $78.
  • Janumet - diabetes, Merck (MRK), 2024 revenues ~935m - price cut from $526, to $80.
  • Otezla - psoriatic arthritis, Merck (MRK), 2024 revenues ~2.13bn - price cut from $4.7k, to $1.65k.
  • Ozempic, Rybelsus and Wegovy - Novo Nordisk (NVO), 2024 revenues ~$25bn, price cut from $959, to $274.

Eagle Eyed readers might have spotted that the CMS has negotiated down the price of Wegovy, despite the fact that earlier this month, Novo Nordisk separately agreed, as part of its MFN deal, that the prices of Ozempic and Wegovy would fall from $1,000 and $1,350 per month, respectively, to $350 when purchased through TrumpRx.

A cynic might suggest that attacking drug pricing in the U.S. is a popular political policy, and that despite the impressive seeming numbers quoted above, not a great deal changes, and establishing the true price of a drug remains elusive.

That would not be entirely fair, however - drug pricing negotiations have traditionally been secretive, nuanced, and arguably not patient-centric, hence government involvement may be viewed as a plus, even if sensible policy decisions may be sacrificed in favor of positive media coverage.

In a statement released by the CMS today, Secretary Robert F. Kennedy, head of the HHS, was quoted stating:

President Trump directed us to stop at nothing to lower health care costs for the American people. As we work to Make America Healthy Again, we will use every tool at our disposal to deliver affordable health care to seniors

Dr Mehmet Oz, M.D., the CMS' administrator, commented:

This year’s results stand in stark contrast to last year’s. Using the same process with a bolder direction, we have achieved substantially better outcomes for taxpayers and seniors in the Medicare Part D program — not the modest or even counterproductive ‘deals’ we saw before.

The CMS' release also noted:

  • Between January 1, 2024, and December 31, 2024, about 5.3 million people with Medicare Part D coverage used these drugs to treat a variety of conditions, such as cancer, type 2 diabetes, and asthma.
  • These selected drugs accounted for about $42.5 billion in total gross covered prescription drug costs under Medicare Part D, or about 15%, during that period.

The price cuts affect Medicare - i.e., government sponsored healthcare for seniors in the U.S. - although the Trump administration has suggested the cuts will create savings of ~$12bn (if 2027 prices were applied today), versus an estimated $6bn saving generated by the previous cuts under the Biden administration.

What Today's Cuts May Mean For Your Pharma Portfolio

Pharma companies have tried to fight off the impact of both IRA and MFN in the courts, and in the media, but the government has been viewed as the victor in both sets of negotiations, even if there are negotiations within negotiations - for example, by agreeing to MFN and to manufacturing more products in the U.S., companies like Novo, Lilly, AstraZeneca and Pfizer have exempted themselves from tariffs on imports of pharma products into the U.S.

The Van Eck pharmaceutical ETF (PPH) is unmoved today, up <01.%, but it is up 19% year-to-date, and 18% on a six-month basis, which tells us that the Pharma industry sector seems to have been broadly unaffected by today's news.

In terms of individual Pharmas, once again, we can make the case that Novo Nordisk is hardest hit by the latest list of fifteen. The Danish Pharma has had a torrid year - stock is down 54%, and 45% year-to-date - but the reality may be that Wall Street has already priced the semaglutide price cuts into Novo's share price and valuation.

As I argued earlier this month, in the long term, thanks to the pedigree of its leading drug, semaglutide, a share price recovery seems the likeliest outcome, and Novo stock did make some slight gains yesterday, after reporting positive data from a Phase 2 study of another GLP-1 agonist drug - this time combined with amylin.

Eli Lilly's tirzepatide escaped the list, although price cuts already agreed in the MFN negotiations mean the company's pricing will be adjusted similarly to Novo's. Potentially, however, the Trump administration's "America First" policies may be a reason why Lilly stock keeps climbing even as Novo's sinks - the Pharma recently attained a >$1 trillion market cap valuation.

Amgen's Otezla and Enbrel accounted for ~10% of the Pharma's revenues in 2024 - >$5bn - however stock is unmoved today - both drugs face patent expiry within the next three years, so in face of the generic competition, the drug's prices may have been slashed anyway. In fact, it is interesting to note - as per Eli Lilly's 2024 10K filing - which drugs' prices the IRA is able to affect:

In 2022, the U.S. government enacted the Inflation Reduction Act of 2022 (IRA). Among other measures, the IRA requires the HHS to effectively set prices for certain single-source drugs and biologics reimbursed under Medicare Part B and Part D.

Generally, these government prices apply beginning at nine years (for medicines approved under a New Drug Application) or thirteen years (for medicines approved under a Biologics License Application) following FDA approval or licensure for the molecule and are set at a price that generally represents a significant discount from existing prices to wholesalers and direct purchasers.

While the law specifies a maximum price that HHS can set, it does not set a minimum price. The Medicare price HHS determines may impact the product’s best price determination under the Medicaid Drug Rebate Program and the 340B Drug Pricing Program, potentially leading to a negative impact on both Medicaid and 340B prices.

I think this summary helps us to understand that recently approved drugs are generally still safe from government price controls - in fact, it is likely the reason tirzepatide does not feature, given it was first approved in ~2022.

AstraZeneca now has two drugs subject to IRA price controls, that accounted for ~20% of revenues in 2024, yet the Pharmas share price is also not down today. GSK, another UK based company, has two drugs on the overall list, accounting for ~12% of its total revenues in 2024, but again, shares appear unaffected.

Even in the case of Linzess, marketed and sold by AbbVie and partner Ironwood (IRWD), Ironwood stock is in fact up >20% today, as the market had believed the news would be worse.

Concluding Thoughts: Drug Pricing Is A Smoke & Mirrors Game - Which The Government Is Now Playing, Too

To conclude this post with a perspective on the longer-term effects for government price controls, I would frankly not be overly concerned by today's news.

Hopefully, the end-patient will experience some benefit from the new price cuts, and from the old also, and it's important to regularly remind Pharma companies that they cannot dictate prices to the general public, in my view.

As discussed, drug pricing is nuanced, complex, open to negotiation, and constantly changing, and if we maintain the view that capitalism and the market is ultimately a fair system, then we can be reasonably confident that Pharma's valuations will withstand ongoing pressure on drug prices. Some drugs will doubtless continue to be overpriced, and others, potentially underpriced.

I don't see any major winners of losers on the back of today's announcement, other than Novo Nordisk, which seems to have been under siege for most of 2025. While I am long-term bullish on the company on account of semaglutide, it is almost mystifying to see the divergence in share price performance between Novo and Eli Lilly, given they are based on the potential of remarkably similar drugs - tirzepatide, in Lilly's case.

Pfizer is another company I'd add to the "mildly to seriously concerned" list, given its rank underperformance post pandemic, and reliance on Eliquis revenues, although I note that Eliquis loses patent protections in 2026 anyway, so any drop off in revenues is likely already baked into the share price.

Ex-U.S. Pharmas may be slightly more vulnerable to pricing wars than U.S. domiciled ones, especially given President Trump's preference for domestic manufacturing, but there is no sign - yet - that e.g. AstraZeneca, or GSK's, valuations are suffering on today's news, and both companies have formidable pipelines delivering world class drug candidates.

In fact, by my calculation the average 6-monthly gain of the world's 15 largest Pharmas remains >15%, even in the face of multiple headwinds experienced in 2025. I don't see momentum being checked in 2026, and would give the Pharma sector a "clean bill of health" despite today's news - negotiation is key for these companies, and it is a significant strength of the sector, in my view.
